# Chapter 19

[u3dexelo] Dawn came up thin and colorless over the compound like a sheet of tin laid across the sky. The air smelled of wet dust and diesel and pepper smoke from the kitchens. Beyond the glass walls of Don Castellanos’s place the chilli fields held their geometry in the half light, rows of green so bright they looked wrong against the dead land. Floodlines hissed. The leaves moved with a small constant trembling like nerves. Men walked between the cars with headlamps on, making little islands of light on chrome and primer and old paint.

[oi8kzsis] Thirty engines sat waiting in a long staggered line on packed dirt. They were arranged as if for inspection, noses pointed down a service road that vanished into scrub and then mountains. The cars were a museum dragged out into the open and told to fight. There were muscle cars with their flanks scarred and welded, sports cars low as knives, rat-rods with exposed lungs of tubing and belts. Some wore matte wrap like tactical gear. Some wore the original lacquer, polished like a prayer. The sound of tools clicking and ratchets turning moved along the line like insects.

[6auve5iq] Flynn walked the Charger once, slow, hand on the roof as if to keep it from floating away. The bodywork still held the collector’s careful restoration under the dust they’d earned on the road. It made him think of theft in a clean room, of white gloves, of alarms that lied after you cut their throats. He checked the tires and the lug nuts and the straps on the fuel cans in the trunk. Diesel had insisted on redundancy, on quiet spares that didn’t depend on anybody’s promise. He popped the hood and looked at the engine like he could read guilt in the casting marks.

[xa5pwc97] Diesel was already under her Chevelle with her feet sticking out, boots planted and shifting, a wrench rising and falling. When she rolled out she didn’t look at him right away. She wiped her hands on her pants and squinted toward the horizon where the first sun made the guard towers throw long shadows. She looked like she’d slept in ten-minute bites. The skin around her eyes had dried tight from the road. She nodded at Flynn’s open hood and came over without a word.

[151i8jf0] She leaned in, listened, touched a hose clamp and tightened it half a turn and then a quarter back like she was tuning an instrument. “It’ll hold,” she said. Her voice was low enough that the men nearby couldn’t steal it. She pointed at the fuel line and then at the extra quick-coupler they’d rigged. “If you have to bail, you don’t unbolt anything. You pull. You run. You understand.” Flynn nodded because nodding was easier than explaining that he understood too well. Her eyes held him for a second and then let him go like a hand opening.

[9pbnsba1] Cage moved through the line like he owned the dirt under the tires. He wore a plain shirt dark with sweat already. His hair was damp and pushed back. People watched him without meaning to, heads turning as he passed. The Shelby sat a few cars up with its hood open, the engine bay clean and mean, like a heart exposed for worship. Cage’s hands were deep in it, fingers on the throttle linkage, and he smiled as if the machine had told him a joke only he could hear. Birdie hovered close, holding a rag and a bottle of water like an acolyte.

[cero3hrj] Birdie kept talking, words spilling out and falling into the roar that wasn’t there yet. He talked about the checkpoints, about the prize car, about how the old racers did it before GPS, about how the cops would be too scared to chase them here. He called Don Castellanos a king and then corrected himself and called him a saint. Cage didn’t answer. He only nodded sometimes, eyes on the bolts, on the belts, on the thin margin between a clean run and a fire. Flynn watched Birdie’s face when Cage did not look at him. The hunger there made Flynn feel old.

[bh5yu4m0] The other racers were ghosts given bodies by their machines. Mateo stood by a black Mustang with a big hood scoop and a stance like it wanted to eat the road. He had a shaved head and a neck thick with old scar tissue. His eyes moved in short mechanical cuts. He didn’t laugh. He didn’t speak. He checked his gloves and his radio and then sat on the fender like he was waiting for a door to open in a wall. Yuki was beside a yellow RX-7 with the paint sun-faded to a sick smile. She had her hair tied tight and a calm face that held nothing for anybody. She made small notes on a thin disposable screen and stuck it to the dash.

[t6trl84j] The Twins arrived together, drifting their matching Camaros into the line with too much throttle and too much music. They wore expensive boots made to look ruined and had sunglasses on in the dark. They slapped hands with strangers like they were at a party. One of them poured something clear into his mouth and made a show of it. Their cars were perfect copies, glossy and bright, the same decals, the same wide tires, but Flynn could see the difference in the way their hands held tools. They weren’t afraid of breaking things because they could buy replacements. Here that kind of comfort was its own weakness.

[ai2v0qsm] Santos was the opposite. He was older than the others by decades, his hair white under a hat, his face cut by sun and years and narrow escapes. He drove an Impala that had been repainted so many times the edges were thick as bark. He moved slow and sure, like a man who had learned that speed begins before the engine starts. When he spoke it was to thank the crew serving coffee, to ask if someone needed tape, to smile at Flynn as if he’d seen his kind before. Flynn felt ashamed for how much he wanted that smile to mean safety.

[av68zbod] Don Castellanos came out when the sun finally cleared the far hills. He walked with two guards a step behind him, both carrying rifles like ceremonial objects. The Don wore linen that stayed white in the dust. His hair was silver and carefully cut. He looked like a man who had never hurried and never needed to. Around him the compound’s staff moved with practiced quiet, setting tables, lifting lids, pouring water. The chilli fields threw a green light up into his face. He raised a hand and the murmuring along the line died down the way animals go still when a bigger animal arrives.

[n9i1cgk4] He spoke in Spanish and then in English without changing his tone. He welcomed them to his land and called them racers like it was a title. He walked the line and touched a few fenders with his fingertips, a collector recognizing his inventory. When he reached the Shelby he stopped. His eyes moved over it with an intimacy that made Flynn’s stomach tighten. “My father’s car,” he said, almost softly. “I am glad it will run again.” Cage leaned back from the engine bay and wiped his hands on his shirt. “We’ll make it legendary,” he said. The Don smiled as if that was exactly what he’d paid for.

[9llsiu2l] They brought the prize out then, making it a thing of daylight. A blood-red ’71 ’Cuda sat under a tarp and when they pulled the tarp away the paint caught the sun and threw it back hard. The car looked untouched by the years, a weapon preserved in a vault. Beside it was a plain black case set on a table. A guard opened it. Stacks of cash sat inside like bricks. Flynn heard someone whistle and then stop himself. Cage stared at the ’Cuda and didn’t blink. For a moment his face went empty and holy. Flynn saw not desire but devotion, like the car was proof that something worth killing for still existed.

[rl9n75g3] The Don explained the route with a map projected onto a portable screen held up by a kid with both hands. Five checkpoints across five hundred miles through backcountry, dirt and broken pavement, climbs into mountains where the air went thin and the road fell away, valleys where the heat cooked the tires. You had to touch all five in order. Anything else was between you and God. “Racing, not murder,” the Don said, and some of the men laughed because they’d come too far to pretend. “But I cannot control what happens at two hundred kilometers per hour.” He gestured at the sky where a helicopter moved, small and steady. “Medical will follow. We cannot guarantee your survival.”

[qt62ihil] They handed out trackers the size of coins and made each driver press it to the dash so it would stick. Checkpoint proof. A little eye. Flynn took his and felt its cheap warmth like something alive. A kid in a Don Castellanos shirt walked past with a bucket of wristbands and zip-tied one to Flynn’s arm, green plastic biting his skin. Beside him a man with tattoos of saints tested a flare gun and laughed with his friends. Another racer strapped a tourniquet to his thigh like it was just another belt.

[1anh1324] Flynn laid his gear on the Charger’s hood: goggles, a cheap helmet with the foam worn thin, gloves still stiff from sweat and dust. He had no radio worth trusting, only a battered handset Diesel had tuned to their channel and a second one in case it drowned. He slid a water bladder under the seat and taped a knife to the console where his hand could find it without looking. None of it made him feel safer. It made him feel like a man packing for a drowning.

[tr7105gy] The racers checked each other the way dogs do, circling, sniffing, pretending not to care. Flynn caught fragments of languages and heard the same words repeated in different mouths: checkpoint, fuel, dust, tires, climb, bridge. One man claimed there was a washed-out culvert at mile eighty that would swallow a car if you hit it wrong. Another said the Don’s people had filled it and that was a lie to spook the rich boys. The lies moved as fast as the truth and no one bothered to separate them.

[81ipzz1k] Cage stood apart from the talk, already somewhere else. He had Birdie buckle his harness tight and then loosen it a notch and then tighten it again, like he was teaching him pain by increments. Birdie’s hands shook and he smiled anyway. Cage looked down the line at Mateo’s Mustang and at Yuki’s low bright car and at the Twins’ glossy copies and he grinned like he’d been offered a menu. Flynn saw Diesel watching him from the side, face blank, fingers flexing as if she wanted to crush something.

[53e8ntwz] Diesel found Flynn at the Charger and stood close like she was blocking the wind. “If it goes bad,” she said. “Get away from him. Promise me.” She didn’t say Cage’s name. The name sat between them anyway, heavy and obvious. Flynn felt the promise form in his mouth like a lie and he hated himself for it and still he said it. “I promise.” Diesel studied him as if she could see the shape of the future on his skin. Then she looked past him and her jaw tightened. Birdie was walking toward the Shelby with a bag slung over his shoulder.

[zppnb7g0] Cage had let Birdie choose. Or Cage had made him choose without touching him. Birdie climbed in on the passenger side like he was climbing into a story he’d been told his whole life. He grinned back at Flynn through the window and his eyes were bright and afraid and begging to be approved. Flynn opened his mouth and nothing came out. He wanted to say get out, he wanted to say you don’t owe him anything, he wanted to say you are not a shield. Cage shut the door from the outside, gentle as a father tucking a kid in. Flynn understood then. Birdie wasn’t there to share the thrill. Birdie was there to keep Flynn and Diesel in line.

[tpnyk1n9] Flynn sat in the Charger and watched the Shelby’s brake lights blink once, a small red wink, and then go dark. He tried to imagine Birdie’s hands on the dash when the road got rough, his knees knocking, his mouth open in a shout that the engine would swallow. He tried to imagine Cage turning his head and saying something kind and meant. He couldn’t. Cage only did kindness the way you used oil, to make friction look like love. Flynn felt the implant behind his ear give a small pulse, a wellness ping asking if he wanted hydration metrics. He ignored it. His throat was dry anyway. He looked at Diesel and she looked back for a heartbeat and in that look he saw a door closing. Not locked. Just closing, slow and quiet, until it met the frame.

[74zhgs90] They rolled to the starting line in a slow crawl, engines waking one by one until the air turned thick with exhaust and heat. The sound hit Flynn’s chest and made his teeth feel loose. Thirty cars idling like a pack, each one shaking against its own leash. Dust rose in sheets and hung around the tires. Men shouted to each other and slapped hoods and leaned out windows. Flynn sat alone in the Charger, hands on the wheel, feeling the vinyl warm under his palms. Diesel was three cars over, eyes forward, mouth set. Cage and Birdie were ahead, the Shelby’s rear end squatting, ready.

[j7rr8cb7] Don Castellanos stood on a platform with a green flag rolled in his fist. Behind him the chilli fields ran to the horizon, and beyond them the land waited, empty and savage and indifferent. The Don lifted his hand and a hush came down as if someone had cut the world’s power. Flynn could hear his own breathing and the tick of the Charger’s cooling system and somewhere far off a bird crying. The Don began the count in Spanish and then in English, his voice calm like a priest at a funeral. Ten. Nine. Eight. At three Flynn’s foot found the pedal and held. At two Diesel’s engine rose. At one Cage’s Shelby barked like an animal. The flag snapped open. Green. And the line broke loose into the desert.
